data.title: nCino: Revenue Growth Acceleration Should Happen In The Coming Years (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on nCino Inc. (NASDAQ:NCNO) was an upgrade to a hold rating because there were early signs of a turnaround in mortgage churn, growing progress with winning large client deals, and a clear path to NCNO monetizing its AI capabilities. I upgrade NCNO to buy. AI Banking Advisor is gaining meaningful adoption and accelerating the shift to the new pricing model, while the mortgage business has flipped from headwind to tailwind. Add to this solid early traction in credit unions and a solid start to international expansion, and I believe the growth profile looks much stronger today.

AI And Platform Pricing

Previously, I pointed out NCNO AI targets as encouraging but ultimately thought it was a “show me story,” given that monetization was still in its infancy. This is no longer the case. The AI-powered Banking Advisor toolset has gone from an early proof-of-concept type of product to one that has gained real commercial traction, as evidenced by adoption from more than 80 customers. What makes this important is not just the revenue attached to Banking Advisor, but how AI is accelerating the transition—the customer base is moving to the new platform pricing model. We know this is happening since management has been clear that customer interest in Banking Advisor is pulling forward renewal conversations, which basically tells us this Banking Advisor AI is a powerful incentive for banks to migrate sooner than expected.

I see this as a game-changing dynamic. Previously, the timeline was a linear four-year renewal cycle, which meant the shift off the legacy seat-based model would be gradual, and so would the impact on the P&L. Now, AI is essentially compressing that timeline, allowing NCNO to realize the benefits of the new model much earlier in the P&L. Notably, the economics are extremely meaningful. On an apples-to-apples basis, management targets a 10% uplift in pricing as customers transition, plus a durable 2–3% annual tailwind from asset growth. My view is that this transition momentum will only get stronger from here because the AI Banking Advisor value proposition is simply too great. For perspective, the Banking Advisor reduces processes like credit memo narratives and mortgage data validation from 20–30 minutes down to seconds, which means employees can focus on higher-value tasks.

In terms of runway, with 21% of annual contract value [ACV] already transitioned, there is still substantial runway for NCNO to ride on this transition tailwind. This should help to drive meaningful growth acceleration, which should lift the stock sentiment.

Mortgage and Growth Initiatives

The mortgage business, which was a drag previously, has seen its turnaround progressing very nicely, and it is one that I think serves as another powerful tailwind to accelerate growth. For reference, in 2Q26, US mortgage subscription revenues grew 22% y/y to $20.9 million. This demand strength is particularly noteworthy given the soft macro backdrop—rates were still arguably high at high-6% in 2Q26. What also makes this so important is that the growth is entirely execution-driven. The growth came from higher volumes tied to large Independent Mortgage Banker [IMB] and homebuilder customers signed during the downturn, which suggests NCNO is taking meaningful market share (mortgage origination was down). Looking ahead, this tailwind should get stronger as the Feds have recently cut rates, and with mortgages down, refinancing activities have surged. This comes at a time when NCNO is transitioning to the new volume-based pricing model, which should help NCNO to capture more upside as activity returns. We also know that the momentum has continued through 3Q26 since management had the confidence to raise its FY26 outlook for the segment from flat to roughly 5% growth.

NCNO also has the right growth initiatives in place that serve as another growth accelerator. One of the most compelling ones is the push into the credit union market, which is made possible as NCNO’s consumer lending product has gotten a lot better. Consumer lending is an important aspect of credit union operations, as for credit unions, the ability to land with one solution like consumer or small business lending and then expand across their entire institution by standardizing on a single platform is a powerful value proposition. As NCNO management explained, a commercial client might apply for a car loan, and the bank "wouldn't have any idea who you are" because they are in a different system. By standardizing on a single platform like NCNO, a credit union can get a 360-degree view of their end customer, linking all their data in one place. The early traction is impressive, as NCNO has already seen six new logos and 35 cross-sells in 2Q25 alone, which is a very encouraging signal that NCNO offerings are well received and it is able to win share from incumbents.

Elsewhere, international execution, which NCNO has not executed well in the past, is also showing credible signs of improvement. The first win in Spain is particularly significant. I see this as a strategic beachhead, wherein by landing a customer (this is a non-bank lender), it validates the platform locally, and this gives NCNO “ammunition,” leverage, and references to accelerate growth in the region. As per management, continental Europe is a >$4 billion opportunity, so I am very positive about this win in Spain.

Valuation

I believe there is a good line of sight to NCNO accelerating revenue growth here, so long as it doesn’t mess up on executing the transition to the new pricing model. Management guided for ~9% y/y revenue growth in FY26; using that as a baseline, I forecast growth to accelerate by ~300 bps a year back to 15% by FY28, which is not a high bar since FY22-FY24 saw higher growth rates. Note that the AI Banking Advisors have compressed the timeline for transition, so we could see a sizeable chunk of growth coming online in the near term. As growth accelerates back to mid-teens, I think it makes sense for multiples to go back to FY24 levels as well. Assuming 6.2x forward revenue, I think the share price has a 40% upside from here.

Risk

Fundamentally, NCNO is dependent on Salesforce, with key solutions for client onboarding and loan origination built on Salesforce's technology. While their agreement extends through January 2031, any negative change in this relationship would materially impact the business. In addition, NCNO faces the risk of potential customer churn from consolidation in the financial industry and also more competition as NCNO expands into new verticals like consumer lending. Internally, the shift to a new asset-based pricing model carries the risk of disruptions.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for NCNO is now a buy. NCNO is no longer just a “show me story.” Its AI monetization, mortgage share gains, and new growth drivers should drive accelerating growth in the years to come. With growth poised to reaccelerate back to mid-teens and upside from the compressed pricing transition timeline, I see potential for a 40% share price gain from here.

data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend REITs That Could Set You Free
data.description:
Few investments provide as much and as reliable an income as real estate.

Your tenants pay rent checks on a monthly basis, resulting in highly consistent and predictable income that generally also rises over time.

But the downside of real estate is that it is not passive.

You first need to find the right property. Then you need to negotiate with the seller. Get financing. Do some renovations. Find a tenant. Manage the property. Etc.

It is a lot more work than most people realize.

Fortunately, there is a solution to earn a completely passive income from real estate, and that is through REITs (VNQ).

REITs are publicly listed real estate investment firms that allow you to invest in real estate just like how you would invest in any other industry - through the purchase of stock.

Moreover, REITs must pay out 90% of their taxable income to shareholders in the form of dividends, resulting in steady high income. While most pay their dividend quarterly, there are quite a few that pay on a monthly basis.

Finally, best of all, REITs are today trading at historically low valuations, which is resulting in relatively high dividend yields.

These days, my REIT investments generate enough monthly passive income for me to sustain myself.

In what follows, I will highlight three of my Top Picks today:

Agree Realty (ADC)

Agree Realty is a net lease REIT, just like Realty Income (O), targeting service-oriented properties such as grocery stores, quick-service restaurants, and home improvement stores.

Such properties earn highly consistent income because their lease terms are very long at over 10 years, annual rent escalations, and tenants are typically responsible for all property expenses, including even the maintenance.

But most investors ignore Agree Realty and will instead buy Realty Income because it is far bigger and better known. It also offers a higher yield and trades at a lower valuation:

Agree Realty Realty Income
P/FFO 16x 14x
Dividend Yield 4.3% 5.4%

I think that this is a mistake.

Yes, Agree Realty is priced at a premium and offers a lower yield, but there are good reasons for it.

It owns better assets, has lower leverage, and enjoys faster growth prospects, all of which justify a higher multiple.

Agree Realty Realty Income
Tenants with an investment-grade rating ~70% ~35%
Debt-to-EBITDA 3.1x 5.5x
Long-term expected annual growth 6% 4%

This has resulted in much higher returns over the past decade as the faster growth compounds over time:

Going forward, I expect the faster growth to more than make up for the lower yield, which should result in further outperformance over the long run.

The 4.3% dividend yield may not seem particularly high, but remember that the REIT is also retaining about 30% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth. Therefore, the real shareholder yield is 6.5%. A portion of that simply isn't paid out just yet. It is reinvested to increase the value of the company, which will eventually be reflected in a higher share price and dividend.

We expect about 5-6% annual growth over the long run, which should result in double-digit annual total returns.

Not bad coming from a highly defensive net lease investment with very low leverage.

Modiv Industrial (MDV)

I do not currently own a position in Modiv Industrial, but I have in the past and hold it high on my watchlist for a future potential investment.

The REIT makes a lot of sense for this list because Modiv stands for "Monthly Dividend".

Modiv is an industrial REIT that focuses on manufacturing net lease properties.

Just like Agree Realty, its lease terms are long, with 12 years left on them on average, resulting in highly consistent and predictable income. It also enjoys 2.7% average annual rent escalations, and its tenants are responsible for all property expenses.

Right now, there is a strong push towards greater manufacturing in the US, and the current administration is pushing for that with tariffs and other incentives.

This has the potential to increase the demand for Modiv's properties, and it may also result in additional investment opportunities for the REIT.

Historically, it has been able to buy properties at an 8%+ cap rate, and if the REIT can attain even a slightly higher valuation multiple, it could start raising new equity to accretively expand its portfolio.

Right now, its cost of equity is still too high, and it is slowing down its growth, but my prediction is that as interest rates return to lower levels, more capital will make its way to the REIT sector, and high-yielding REITs like Modiv will get bid up.

This will then allow it to greatly accelerate its future growth. While you wait, you are earning a near-8% dividend yield that's fully covered, and the management has made it their mission to pay reliable monthly income, which signals that they are very unlikely to cut it unless they really have to.

Whitestone REIT (WSR)

Whitestone REIT is the lowest-yielding REIT on this list, and yet, it might be my favorite opportunity because it offers the greatest potential for value creation and long-term upside.

The REIT owns primarily grocery-anchored, service-oriented strip centers in rapidly growing Sunbelt markets like Phoenix and Dallas.

These properties are today undersupplied, resulting in high occupancy rates and steady rent growth.

According to a recent study by Kimco Realty (KIM), rents would need to rise up 65% to make new development projects economically feasible in this sector due to the high construction cost.

Therefore, we expect the 3-5% same-property NOI growth to continue for many years to come. In addition to that, Whitestone is creating additional value by retaining about 40% of its cash flow to acquire additional properties and/or redevelop existing ones.

Its balance sheet is also gradually getting stronger as its cash flow increases, which will eventually justify a higher valuation multiple.

Even then, it is today trading at just 12x FFO and an estimated 30% discount to its net asset value. The REIT has received several offers from private equity companies to buy it out. The last offer was at $15, and the management argued that it undervalued the company.

Today, you can still buy shares at just below $13. While you wait for the upside, you earn a steady 4.2% dividend yield paid monthly.

Closing Note

Our Core Portfolio at High Yield Landlord is today offering a 6.2% average dividend yield, and it includes many monthly paying REITs. Best of all, we achieve this high yield with a low 59% average payout ratio, which leaves a lot of retained cash flow for the REITs to reinvest in growth.

data.title: Corporacion America Airports: Global Growth Story Still Priced As An Argentina Play
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In July, I published an article where I said that the biggest challenge for Corporación América Airports (NYSE:CAAP) was to get rid of the "Argentine risk premium" that the market applies almost by reflex. I argued that the company had ceased to be a simple emerging bet to become a global, profitable and diversified airport platform. Today, after the results of Q2 2025, I feel that this thesis has been strengthened: the fundamentals have been consolidated, but the market still does not recognize them in the valuation.

During the quarter, CAAP again grew in traffic, revenue and EBITDA, with margins that not only remained solid but also increased compared to last year. The most interesting thing is that he did it with a healthier balance sheet than ever: close to $500 MM in cash, total liquidity of $595 MM and a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of just 1.0x.

Despite this clear picture, the stock continues to be quoted with discounts of 40% to 60% compared to its peers. For me, that difference between what the numbers show and what the market discounts is the real entry point for the patient investor, so I maintain the "buy" stance.

Overview

Corporación América Airports (CAAP) operates 52 airports in six countries: Argentina, Italy, Uruguay, Brazil, Ecuador and Armenia. I think it is important to point out that this network gives it something that few operators have: the possibility of combining emerging markets with high growth potential with more mature ones that provide stability. That mix is what allows it to weather external shocks with a degree of resilience difficult to replicate.

In addition, CAAP has been increasingly focused on expanding non-aeronautical revenues, which is key to improving profitability. We saw it in Ezeiza, where the extension of the duty free was inaugurated in May, which went from 700 to 1,100 m2.

In Montevideo, work is developing to improve the passenger experience, and in Brazil, the shopping mall of Brasilia is started, with opening scheduled for 2026.

In Europe, the Florence master plan received environmental approval in April, and expansion of the Yerevan terminal continues in Armenia. All this confirms to me that CAAP sees its airports as commercial platforms and not just transit spaces.

The ability to finance these projects is explained by a very solid balance sheet. With $497 MM in cash at the close of Q2 and a total liquidity of $595 MM, the company can sustain long-term investments without compromising its financial structure.

As Martín Eurnekian, its CEO said,

We delivered a strong second quarter, with broad-based traffic growth, double-digit increases in revenue and Adjusted EBITDA, and meaningful EBITDA margin expansion, reflecting the strength of our diversified portfolio and disciplined execution"

Q2 2025

Q2 2025 made it clear that the air traffic recovery is no longer a rebound, but a new phase of growth. In Q2, CAAP transported 20.7 million passengers, an increase of 13.7% YoY, with historical records in Argentina, Italy, Armenia and Uruguay. Argentina was the big driver, with an increase of 17.3% YoY, but Italy, Brazil and Armenia also showed significant progress.

Consolidated revenue was $435.2 MM, up 18.9% YoY. When I look at the revenue per passenger, I see that it is $21, +4.5% YoY, which confirms that not only traffic is growing, but also the value added by each traveler, especially through commercial revenues. In fact, this line grew 22% YoY thanks to duty free, parking lots, VIP lounges and retail.

Adjusted EBITDA was $167.8MM, with a growth of 23.3% YoY and a margin of 38.6%, 140 bps above the previous year. Argentina led again with an EBITDA +34% YoY, but what stands out is that all markets, except Brazil, showed double-digit gains.

Net profit attributable to shareholders was $49.3 MM, stable compared to Q2 2024, mainly due to FX losses, although this does not affect the operating photo.

Valuation

What surprises me most is that, with this level of results, CAAP still trades at multiples that reflect excessive punishment. The EV/EBITDA FWD is at just 5.5x, less than half of the sector average at 12.6x. The EV/EBIT FWD stands at 7.7x against 17.5x in the industry.

Even the 11.9x P/E GAAP FWD implies a discount close to 50% compared to its peers. And if I look at the PEG FWD, I see 0.71x versus 1.83x of the sector: the market is not even recognizing the expected growth in earnings.

With the stock around $18.75 and a consensus price target of $24.6, I see an upside of the order of 30%. For me, this gap is not justified in the numbers and quality of assets that the company manages.

Growth

The growth profile of CAAP is another spread that, in my opinion, the market underestimates. In Q2, revenues grew 26.9% YoY, compared to just 3.6% of the sector average. Going forward, the consensus expects a 12.8% increase in revenues, more than double the 4.9% of the sector.

In EPS, CAAP projects an annual growth of 16.3%, almost double the 8.2% of industry. Even on a horizon of three to five years, EPS shows an increase of 16.7% CARG against 11.6% of the average. This confirms to me that we are not facing a one-off rebound, but a path of sustainable expansion. To this is added that FCF grew 37.8% YoY, reinforcing the company’s ability to finance its own growth without resorting to additional debt.

Profitability

If valuation and growth already look attractive, when I look at profitability CAAP directly stands out as one of the most efficient players in its universe. The EBITDA margin in Q2 was 34.2%, compared to 14.3% for the sector, which is more than double. The EBIT margin reached 22.4%, against 10.1% in industry.

The most notable is the FCF margin, which reached 22%, while the sectoral average barely exceeds 6.6%. This tells me that CAAP not only generates revenue, but turns it into cash exceptionally. The ROIC was 9.8%, compared to 7.0% of its peers, which confirms that it uses its capital more efficiently.

Risks

In spite of everything, it is clear that not everything is perfect. The point that I think is important to highlight is that CAAP’s exposure to Argentina remains high, as more than 60% of revenues still come from there. The risk of inflation and FX can generate mismatches between costs in pesos and dollar rates.

In addition, the airport business is always subject to external shocks, such as geopolitical crises or changes in the travel cycle.

But what reassures me is that CAAP has shown, even during the pandemic, that it knows how to adapt and preserve margins in adverse contexts.

Conclusion

Q2 2025 again showed that Corporación América Airports not only recovered what was lost after the pandemic, but emerged strengthened, with a more diversified network, expanding margins and a solid balance sheet. The current multiples reflect a punishment that no longer has support in the fundamentals.

While the market continues to look at it as an Argentine proxy, I see it for what it really is: a global, efficient and growing company. For me, that difference between perception and reality is exactly where the opportunity is, so my stance remains "buy".

data.title: American Battery Technology: Speculative Fervor As Trump Administration Considers Stake In Lithium Americas
data.description:
One of the most intense rallies of 2025 has been in the battery and power technology industry. The emerging field is made primarily of smaller, low- or pre-revenue companies that may have a growing total addressable market. In my view, this "industry" includes both companies pursuing new power generation technology, prominently small nuclear reactor firms like Oklo (OKLO), as well as storage technology companies like Solid Power (SLDP).

Although these two types of firms are not directly related, they're both benefiting from hype surrounding increased government support (lower permitting requirements or direct grants/loans), as well as very high demand from the utilities industry. The latter demand driver is rooted in forecasts of much higher US electricity demand from EVs and data centers over the next decade, following about two decades of no power demand growth.

Many stocks in the "battery and power technology" industry have doubled or more this year. To me, this raises the risk of overvaluation, particularly because stock prices are likely to rise on increased media exposure. As such, we must focus on revenue/income potential offset by valuations and risks that these technologies will not be as fruitful as hoped. In many instances, I think excess retail speculative trading is pushing these stocks up, causing them to rise sharply despite no fundamental changes. This issue may be the case for American Battery Technology Company (NASDAQ:ABAT), which is up 425% over the past year:

Solid Power and American Battery are different firms, but I see a strong stock price correlation due to them having a shared narrative surrounding "power storage tech." Solid Power is a solid-state "power storage tech" company that I'm somewhat bullish on.

American Battery is basically a pre-revenue company (it made a few million last year) that is working on a few operations, including lithium battery recycling, "leach extraction" of Nevada (HQ location) claystone to produce lithium. It also has "one of the largest known measured, indicated, and inferred lithium claystone resource deposits in the United States." ABAT has the bare bones for a vertically integrated lithium battery company. However, it lacks the sales and the liquid capital to complete these projects.

Its market cap is now up to $487M, giving it some power, but it is still relatively small. ABAT garners pretty high short interest at 9.9%, with very mixed analyst opinions. There's some risk that a short squeeze is pushing the stock higher as sellers race to cover heavy gains. Those short-term technical trends matter, but ultimately, we must focus on the potential value of its projects and the cost and risk involved in them. Overall, much of this depends on US government policy and support, which is the primary driver for ABAT's recent volatility.

American Battery Opening US Lithium Supply

As an analyst, I try to be careful of companies that market jargon-heavy "potential" operations. By "potential," I mean operational aims the company is working toward, but is still pre-revenue, with an unclear development cost and time estimate. I am also careful with "jargon" terms because these can oversell the opportunity to layperson investors or confuse investors/analysts, such that they overlook the potential (which I see with Oklo and Solid Power).

American Battery explains its technology as, "Our advanced hydro process increases the recovery of critical minerals (Li, Ni, Mn, Co) from end-of-life batteries and enables the cost-effective recovery of lithium from domestic claystone resources." From its investor presentation, the company notes that the US currently produces almost no primary input materials for batteries (a tariff/strategic issue).

The company's operations are limited to its Lithium Hydroxide refining and recycling pilot plant, funded partly by Department of Energy grants. Late last year, it was awarded another $144M grant for a second recycling facility, producing up to 100K tons of "battery metals" per year. Clearly, the US government has shown a strategic interest in expanding its operations.

I describe its business as "getting paid to take old batteries and then selling the materials." In the future, this business should be expanded to include mining and refining of the vast Nevada lithium deposit. However, that is still in the early permitting, design, and financing phase. The Tonopah Flats project is currently estimated to complete permitting and review by 2027, so I'd place this as an early 2030s revenue timeline. By then, we can only hope lithium is more valuable.

Why Lithium is Not a Good Investment

Over recent years, I've generally held the view that lithium will be a challenging and glut-biased market indefinitely. I took a close look at the industry in early 2024, with a bullish outlook on the Lithium Americas (LAC) project, seeing the Thacker Pass, Nevada complex as potentially having a huge amount of low-cost lithium that could end US import dependence.

The core challenge with lithium is that it has very high upfront investment costs (into mining and refining), but relatively low long-term overhead costs (labor, etc). While that is good on paper, there's a ton of investment into the industry today, meaning supply may significantly overshoot estimates and rise faster than demand over the next decade. Once that initial development is done, it is not very expensive for lithium extraction companies to ramp up if they have the resources.

On ABAT's presentation, they project demand rising above supply by 2028, with stalled supply growth in the 2030s. That is based on 2023 research from Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, which may overlook the surge in investment over the past two years. For example, Thacker Pass is under construction and should ramp up production by 2028. Thacker may have 44.5Mt of lithium carbonate, and has at least 14.3 (proven and probable). Global annual demand is around 1.3Mt, projected to grow to around 4Mt by 2040.

American Battery's Tonopah has 21.15 Mt as measured, indicated, and inferred (upper bound), which is less than Thacker. However, Thacker's is lithium carbonate, while Tonopah's is lithium hydroxide monohydrate "LHM". LHM is more directly useful for batteries due to better solubility, potentially making American Battery's vertically integrated approach more feasible. That said, lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide are currently worth practically the same, just over $9K/mt.

Looking at the Chinese Yuan price, we can see lithium is back in a glut, worth far less than it was at the "high EV demand, lower supply (post COVID)" period of 2022:

For years, I've seen analysts project a lithium shortage, but for the most part, we see a consistent glut. Because EV demand projections are so strong, we see continued heavy investment into lithium supply despite lower prices. There have been some mine closures in China, but its policy remains very supportive. This is now mirrored by immense support from the US government, with Trump's administration seeking an equity stake in Lithium Americas.

This decision is likely the primary catalyst behind ABAT's rally, with investors likely hoping for similar direct financial support. That said, I argue that these companies will really need support on prices. Even with tariffs, it is unclear if US lithium mining or recycling will be profitable at the price levels we've seen outside of temporary shortage periods.

The Earth has a ton of lithium, likely 88Mt. Though a large portion of that is still unproven, we're seeing huge discoveries in Nevada and other areas. The issue is not availability, but cost competition with cheaper South American and Chinese mines. I do not know if that 88Mt figure accounts for the recent US discoveries, which may be over half of that figure, so I suspect not. So, if we see annual demand slowly rise to 4Mt, there are likely enough global reserves to fulfill demand for the next fifty years easily.

American Battery Needs a Lot of Money

Can ABAT and LAC produce at a competitive price point with emerging market countries? Maybe, but that may also depend on tariffs, and I personally would avoid making investments that require significant government financial aid to be feasible. American Battery ended its last quarter without a large liquidity balance, particularly compared to its operating cash flow:

It had about six months of cash to offset its CFO, which is concerning. Its solvency risk is extremely low because of its roughly half-billion market cap, but that means it will likely be heavily diluted. Indeed, its shares outstanding have nearly tripled since 2024, with that trend continuing on a $50M stock offering plan announced last week.

American Battery's investor materials do not present a precise estimate of project development costs and timeline. There is a 2024 report that claims:

With $781.8 million in initial capital costs, production costs of$4,636/ton of LHM, overall operating costs of $6,080/ ton of LHM produced, and average annual production of 33,000 tons of LHM, the report estimates a $9.56 billion after-tax net present value (NPV) at a 5% discount rate.

That report comes from ABAT, so it must be taken with a grain of salt. These projects have a tendency to cost more and take longer than expected. However, even at today's lower prices, this project should be profitable based on its cost estimates. Still, I think that NPV estimate is dependent on previously higher lithium prices. The big issue there is that lithium is extremely volatile, often moving by 50-100% every year due to demand and supply growing very quickly.

The Bottom Line

Overall, I think American Battery is a very interesting company and is worth following, particularly if it receives more direct financial support like Lithium Americas. American Battery has very lofty promises, but how long will it take for investors to see profits, and how much dilution will occur between now and then? Based on its dilution rate, its lack of liquidity, negative cash flows, and meager annualized revenue of ~$10M (though rising very quickly).

The lithium demand narrative makes sense, but to me, it is offset by the fact that lithium is far from a rare material. Until we see a lithium shortage, I think it's best to avoid betting on one, particularly given the many ongoing mining projects. If we take away all potential and existing government support (grants, tariffs, peer equity investments), I'd probably be bearish on ABAT because of cost/supply competition with China/South America.

However, I am neutral because we're seeing decent bipartisan support for US lithium and battery/power technology companies. Although the ~$487M market cap is high compared to its ~ five-year sales outlook, that is a small "drop in the bucket" for the US government. In my opinion, we're seeing ABAT surge on hope for more direct government support, with speculation and potentially a short squeeze boosting returns. As a general rule, I avoid stocks that are in speculative fervor, even if that is partially grounded on real catalysts (in this case, the LAC equity investment). To me, it's "overvalued and overhyped", but if the government gets more involved, it can easily rise much higher.

data.title: Precision Drilling: At The Starting Gate
data.description:
Introduction

Precision Drilling Corporation (NYSE:PDS) (TSX:PD:CA) is a top-tier Canadian land oil and gas well driller with the largest market share in the country. Drilling has been more resilient in Canada than in the U.S. thanks to new demand coming from its emerging West Coast export market. It also works in the key U.S. basins- the Marcellus, and the Haynesville, in particular, where it's high technology rigs are in demand due to extreme drilling conditions.

PDS has outperformed the land drilling cohort substantially since the first of the year. Guidance from its Q2 analyst call, where they surprised the market with a strong quarter in what traditionally is their weakest-spring thaw, due to many oilfields being inaccessible, was also bullish for the rest of 2025.

PDS has rebounded strongly off its April lows into the upper $50s, which is in the low end of its 5-year range between the low $40s and the mid $80s. Analysts have the company ranked as an Overweight with price targets between $56-$87.00. The company notched $85.00 in 2022, in a substantially higher oil price environment than exists currently, but margins have improved thanks to technology, so there may be a pathway to the upper range of estimates. The median is $72 which would put the company at the top of its range for the last couple of years. Can increasing utilization and higher margins deliver anything beyond the median?

Perhaps. Expectations are for an EPS print of $0.99, which is 4X Q2, but well behind Q3, of 2024 ($1.68). A print of $1.13 is anticipated for Q4, and $1.55 for Q1, 2026 reflecting their strongest traditional calendar period, lies just ahead. Both metrics lag comparisons for 2024, but that sets the stage for a beat in the coming quarters that could propel the stock to the top of expectations early next year. Narrowed WCS differentials and increased offtake capacity in the Canadian market, and stronger gas prices in the U.S. market could be just the setup for continued outperformance by PDS.

The land drilling macro

It's tough right now as the dismal performance of the land driller cohort suggests in the comparison graph above. That said on the U.S. side, we have seen declines in the rig count stabilize on the oil side in recent weeks, and gas drillers have reactivated about 19 rigs since May. This looks like a tradable bottom to me. In that case we should see continued strength in gas rigs, and if oil can remain where it is, we should see more rigs returning to work. On the Canadian side we should see a continued rally toward the 250 level that we see typically in the first quarter. Currently Canada has just under 200 rigs turning to the right. That would be only a 70% fleet utilization, so there's still room for more rigs to head to work if prices improve. Land rigs can usually be mobilized quickly-subject to crew availability. So there are reasons for optimism in this category from current levels above and below the 49th parallel.

The thesis for PDS

The company is a large contract driller with a very loyal customer base in Canada and the U.S. PDS also has a small international footprint in the MEA area with 5 rigs in Kuwait and 2 in Saudi Arabia. It has built a reputation of being among the best in the field, with Super Spec walking rigs that save time moving from well to well on a multi-well pad. Called Super Triples for the number of joints of pipe that can be assembled in the derrick, these rigs feature advanced AI-led automation that improves efficiency, as well as the high hook load capacity, solids handling capacity, and high pressure mud pumps need to drill the three and four mile laterals the industry prefers in shale.

As of their last call, the company had 36 of these rigs in U.S. basins, an increase of 9 from Q1 levels. PDS also has a good market in the Super Singles and Super Doubles that are used in the heavy oil areas, and Montney areas respectively. Only the competition sensitive Super Doubles are seeing slacker demand.

The company is cutting costs, and tied $25 mm of upgrade capex to new contracts with improved day rates. The company is prioritizing 2025 debt reduction-$91 mm retired as of midyear, and share repurchase-$45 mm retired this year, targets.

PDS cited customer demand as supporting increased utilization across product lines, and a particular strong area in heavy oil country. This demand is supporting rig upgrades and their Evergreen Solutions technology providing a catalyst for 10 rig upgrades currently, and expandable to another 26 rigs during the year. These are priced as options and payout very quickly.

CEO Kevin Neveu was optimistic about the market conditions in the call-

Second quarter results were stronger than we anticipated with excellent free cash flow and better-than-expected margins. We locked in additional term contracts in the United States and Canada, and we experienced strong customer demand for our Super Triple rigs in every gas basin in North America, all this coupled with continued customer demand for our pad-equipped Super Single operating in Canadian heavy oil and thus opening opportunities to invest in further rig enhancements, providing revenue and earnings growth opportunities for Precision.

Possible catalysts for PDS

PDS is bullish on gas thanks to the new demand coming from LNG Canada, and thinks that the key Canadian gas play in the Montney is on the rise, in part because of the liquids fractions that come with the gas. PDS notes full capacity in LNG Canada driving up to 5 additional rigs. They referred to a large customer's assessment of the play that coincides with their own.

Now recently, one of our largest customers at their Investor Day referred to the Montney as a world-class gas play, but with the most remaining oil inventory of any play in North America. This clearly aligns with Precision's view of the Montney and provides long-term visibility for rig demand in this play.

Like other service providers, PDS in very active in the Haynesville and Marcellus plays, and noted the potential for new rig activations driven by increasing LNG gas demand.

Customer interest in the Haynesville and Marcellus is encouraging, we have ongoing customer discussions for potential rig activations late this year and into 2026. There's no question that LNG export capacity additions and data center power demand expectations are driving customer sentiment for natural gas operators.

Q2 2025 and guidance

PDS beat guidance on EPS-$0.76, and revenue- by $5.71 mm, for the quarter. PDS revenue of $407 million, was a stepdown of 5% from Q2 2024, and off 15% from Q1's $455 mm-CAD. EBITDA of $108 mm was enhanced by $7 mm of customer paid upgrades. U.S. rig operating margins improved by $666 per day, with net margin of $9,026 exceeding guidance of $7-8,000 per day. Canadian margins also improved by $882 to $15,306 per day that included upfront payments from customers of $1,440. For Q3 they expect margins between $12-13,000 per day.

Capital expenditures for the quarter were $53 million, including $27 million for upgrade and expansion and $26 million for maintenance and infrastructure. Their full year 2025 capital plan has been increased from $200 million to $240 million and is comprised of $150 million for sustaining and infrastructure and (customer supported) $86 million for upgrade and expansion.

Q2 cash flow supported debt reduction of $74 million and share repurchases of $14 million. As of June 30, their long-term debt position net of cash was approximately $644 million.

Company filings.

Your takeaway

PDS has the strongest three quarters of the fiscal year remaining before them. At current prices the company at an attractive and very competitive EV/EBITDA multiple of 3.5X NTM. Given the relative price performance of the company, the 3.5X seems a little conservative given what we have established are their reasonable prospects over the next few quarters. A beat on upcoming earnings (expected October 27th) could easily ignite a continued rally in the stock.

If the third quarter comes in as expected at $0.99 USD per share, EBITDA of ~$400 mm USD could be generated on an NTM basis. Their current 3.5X multiple would equate to a $100 share price. This blows past the top estimates put out by the analysts, but I think their current multiple is pretty conservative, particularly if we see any acceleration of commodity prices.

Bottom line PDS is a top tier drilling contractor trading at an attractive multiple compared with competitors. Its market dominance in Canada with it increased demand and margin expansion put a buy target on the company. That said, it's trading above its 50 day SMA of $54, after three months of strong rally. While we think PDS is a buy we are inclined to watch for an entry point below the 50 day SMA.

data.title: Uranium Energy's Stock Continues To Rise, But Earnings Are Not Keeping Up
data.description:
Back in January, I initiated coverage on Uranium Energy Corporation (NYSE:UEC). I rated them a hold because while they had a lot of potential for becoming a major US-based uranium producer, I believed the price was a bit too high. Then this happened.

[object HTMLElement]

With the stock having nearly doubled since then, you’d think they were firing on all cylinders and destroying earnings estimates. In reality, they have missed every single quarterly EPS estimate since then, and this latest quarterly release on Wednesday saw them miss badly on revenue estimates as well. Something is wrong here, because the stock isn’t dropping as a result of performance, indeed after the pre-opening earnings miss, Uranium Energy’s stock was up the better part of the day anyhow, only closing marginally down from where they opened. A big miss and the market shrugged.

I think it’s necessary, at this point, to take another look at Uranium Energy, to see what is happening with the stock performance, and whether it’s really something investors should take a closer look at.

Hype Springs Eternal, Prices Lag

Between their website and the conference call, you’d think Uranium Energy was anointed as the most exciting and consequential energy company in the world. The narrative is that the whole world is uniformly behind nuclear energy and that demand for uranium that only they can provide is soaring because of energy demands.

On paper it makes a measure of sense. I went over the case for Uranium Energy back in January and a lot of the business model is based on the idea that there is, or at least will be, growing demand for uranium because there is indeed a growing interest in nuclear energy.

Here’s where the problem starts though: the price of uranium just isn’t matching the narrative. The US banned Russian imports of uranium back in 2024, but in practice almost all the uranium consumed in the US still comes from Russia by way of waivers. Those waivers are meant to end in 2027, but whether or not that happens may depend heavily on demand and the ability of alternative sources to supply what the US needs.

Uranium isn’t traded on the Comex like a regular commodity, but the price for it has not tracked generally upward in spite of the talk of anticipated soaring demand. To the contrary, uranium prices in 2025 have been substantially lower than they were in 2024. Indeed, it is only in the past week that uranium got back over $80/lb, which is still far from the levels we saw in early to mid 2024. We’ll see now how the weak prices have continued to hurt the bottom line for Uranium Energy.

Consistent Misses Throughout 2025

This brings us to Wednesday’s miss. They came in not only behind quarterly EPS estimates once again, but for the full year they missed substantially on revenue estimates. That is, in no small part, because uranium prices just weren’t there.

2023

2024

2025

Revenue

$164 million

$0.2 million

$67 million

Gross Profit

$49.7 million

$37 thousand

$24.5 million

Operating Income

$8.9 million

($56.4 million)

($73.3 million)

Comprehensive Income

($6.7 million)

($39.4 million)

($88.2 million)

GAAP EPS

(1¢)

(7¢)

(20¢)

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

Not only were they not profitable, they actually lost more money than they did in 2024, when they sold almost no uranium at all. 2024 saw much higher prices than 2025 did, but Uranium Energy was unable to take advantage of that, and continues to pin its hope on growing capacity and a future with even higher prices that may or may not ever arrive.

Low Cost and Growing Capacity

Not that things are all bad. As mentioned in the conference call, Uranium Energy is producing uranium at a very low cost, which is a very good sign. The total cost per pound was put at $36.41. With prices in excess of $80/lb, there is certainly reason to be hopeful the company can approach break-even overall as their capacity grows.

And the capacity is growing. The Burke Hollow project in Texas is almost done, with the company expecting it to be ready in November and to begin producing in December. Assuming things go right, that’s another mine in operation, and more opportunity to increase revenue. Again, that assumes the prices remain high, and they certainly didn’t last year.

The underlying assumption is that growing uranium demands will lead to higher prices, but demand has been growing for awhile now, and the prices aren’t directly tracking that, likely in part because the supply chain is overwhelmingly overseas right now, and without a commodity exchange for uranium, companies that need uranium are contracted out to individual producers. Uranium Energy’s goal is to replace those producers.

It’s an ambitious plan. The company has stated its goal to be an end-to-end supplier of uranium, mining it out of the ground, refining it, and converting it for various customers. If successful, that could be a very lucrative business, but it’s not going to be cheap. That’s where another problem comes in.

Dilution: UEC’s Capacity Grows, But So Do Shares Outstanding

Uranium Energy makes a big deal of having a strong balance sheet, a lot of working capital and no real debt. You might be wondering how, being consistently unprofitable for years and years, they managed that. To see how, we have to look at the statement of cash flow.

2024

2025

Operating FCF

($106 million)

($64 million)

Investing FCF

($24.6 million)

($157 million)

Financing FCF

$173 million

$285 million

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

As you can see, Uranium Energy isn’t operating cash flow positive. Investing FCF losses reflect expenses in property and plants, and in 2025 a $179 million expense for the acquisition of Sweetwater in Wyoming, which we discussed in the January article. The hugely positive financing FCF number is the part that warrants some serious attention, because it’s materially entirely proceeds from issuing new shares of UEC, hundreds of millions of dollars annually. For a more direct look at that, here’s the number of shares outstanding as of respective 10-Ks.

2022 10-K

345.8 million shares

2023 10-K

385.8 million shares

2024 10-K

411.4 million shares

2025 10-K

464.7 million shares

Uranium Energy is issuing a lot of new shares, and while some of that goes to acquiring assets like Sweetwater, the reality is that a lot of it is simply going to CapEx and day-to-day operations for a company that has yet to reach break-even.

The goal of being an end-to-end uranium producer is not an inexpensive one, and even if the potential rewards are high, I would expect Uranium Energy to have to spend a lot more money developing the capacity to refine and convert uranium. Subsequently, I don’t think we can safely rule out further dilution, which in the past several years has been substantial.

That brings us to another issue, the premium being asked. After all this dilution, Uranium Energy isn’t exactly awash in assets, even if they’re relatively cash-rich. Here’s the balance sheet:

Cash and Equivalents

$149 million

Total Current Assets

$234 million

Total Current Liabilities

$26.4 million

Total Shareholder Equity

$984 million

(source: most recent 10-K from SEC)

While the current ratio is impressively high, this is a miner commanding a price/book ratio of 6.46, enormously rich in a sector where the median is a mere 1.42.

Unhedged

Uranium Energy is emphasizing that their unhedged strategy maximizes their exposure to uranium price gains. That sounds great on the surface, but as we mentioned before, uranium prices aren’t exactly skyrocketing. They’re exposed to gains if the price goes up, but they’re also just as exposed if the price goes down, and despite demand, that price has been a bit volatile in both directions.

I don’t want to totally discount that Uranium Energy may end up profiting on this strategy, but we need to be fully aware that the exposure can be a double-edged sword, and the high premium the stock is commanding seems to have a lot of anticipated gains priced in that may or may not materialize.

A Price Too Dear, I’d Stay Away

While Uranium Energy continues to have the potential they had when I wrote about them in January, they still aren’t profitable, and the share price has only risen more and more since then, even as the individual shares were diluted to fund growth and operations. What we have is a company with a stock priced for everything going right, and it may well, but I don’t think it’s worth paying this premium for exposure to that.

In the end, I’m downgrading UEC from hold to sell, not that I’d advise looking at it as a potential short by any means, because the momentum behind the stock is high right now, but because if one currently has shares, this seems like an excellent time to start looking at taking some off the table.

Uranium Energy is a fine, ambitious company looking to build something substantial, but I feel that the premium being asked right now is far too high, and too much needs to go right for the company to ever make enough money to justify such a price. Personally, I’d stay away.

data.title: WCBR: Cybersecurity Stocks Setting Up Well Into Q4 (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
CrowdStrike (CRWD) is a popular story stock. The tale was a nightmare, however, in the summer of 2024, when a major operational bug resulted in a worldwide outage. CRWD plunged, but CEO George Kurtz took charge and met with reportedly hundreds of his key customers. His travels appear to have been worth it.

Shares tagged a new all-time high 12 months after the botch. The stock jumped just last week on the heels of a strong guidance update, but has since retreated for four sessions running as of this writing.

CRWD is just one cybersecurity stock, but it’s the largest component of the WisdomTree Cybersecurity Fund ETF (NASDAQ:WCBR). I had a hold rating on the fund almost a year ago. With shares up an impressive 18% since then, I am upgrading WCBR to a buy. I see bullish technical trends, although I remain cautious on the valuation. With a strong YTD performance, I expect the ETF to rally into 2025’s close.

CrowdStrike Shares Pulling Back After A Strong First Half Of September

According to the issuer, WCBR is designed to track the performance of companies primarily involved in providing cybersecurity-oriented products that meet Index eligibility requirements. It can be used by investors to gain targeted exposure to companies driving innovation in cybersecurity technologies and can complement growth or technology holdings.

WCBR is a small ETF with just $136 million in assets under management as of September 23, 2025. That’s up from $107 million at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is moderate at 45 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is very low at just 0.02%.

Share-price momentum has improved markedly since my October 2024 assessment, earning the product a robust A- ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. WCBR is highly risky, however, mainly due to its relative standard deviation measure. A concentrated portfolio is another potential peril if the global cybersecurity narrative wanes.

On liquidity, there are likewise issues to consider. Average daily volume is less than 20,000 shares, while the 30-day median bid/ask spread is wide at 26 basis points. Thus, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market’s open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has heavy exposure to the right side of the style box, indicating its bent to the growth style. There’s just 4% value access. There is, however, considerable size diversification. Risk is also spread out internationally, with a material 14% allocation to ex-US equities. That’s a nice combination, in light of the concentrated wager in a single theme.

Where I remain worried is on the valuation front. The price-to-earnings ratio is high at 32.2x. Pair that with a long-term earnings growth rate of 9.2%, and the resulting PEG ratio is more than three. So, why am I upgrading it to a buy? The technical potential is huge, which I will detail later.

WCBR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As far as the sector breakdown, WCBR is entirely invested in the Information Technology sector. What’s more, the top 10 holdings account for a high 47% of the total portfolio. With earnings season just three weeks away, keeping close tabs on both the fundamentals and technicals is key. CrowdStrike is an off-season reporter, though.

WCBR: Holdings & Dividend Information

For seasonality, I pulled up a view of the largest cybersecurity ETF, the First Trust NASDAQ Cybersecurity ETF (CIBR). The past 10 years of price data reveal that the October through February period has been quite bullish. Thus, the calendar trend shapes up nicely for the industry.

WCBR: Bullish Q4 Trends Set to Ensue

The Technical Take

With a premium valuation and being a thematic wager, WCBR’s technical situation is very interesting from a bulls’ view. Notice in the chart below that shares are currently consolidating in a symmetrical triangle pattern. The presumption is that the fund will break out in the trend of larger degree (up). What’s more, we can calculate an upside measured move price objective of $41 based on the $9 height at the start of the pattern, added onto the breakout point.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average—it's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. The fund held that trendline indicator on a pullback in August. Today, with a high amount of volume by price below the current level, there should be ample support on a pullback. As for the RSI oscillator at the top of the graph, it fell below 40 (which I don’t like to see). Ergo, it’s key for the price breakout to occur with a momentum breakout.

WCBR: Technical Target to $41 In Play, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on WCBR. The valuation is a risk, but technical factors are healthy. Seasonality is also on the bulls’ side.

data.title: Marks and Spencer: Year 3 Of 'Reshape For Growth' And What Comes Next
data.description:
Marks and Spencer Group (OTCQX:MAKSY)(OTCQX:MAKSF)—the century-old UK retailer—is now in year three of its 'Reshape for Growth' turnaround plan. And you only need to look at the share price to see how much the story has changed.

Since it began to be implemented by Stuart Machin, the CEO appointed that year, the stock has skyrocketed from just over $2 to ~$10 (an impressive increase of ~400%!). As soon as it reached this level in mid-2024, it started to move sideways.

So, is it still worth getting in now?

I mean, the big upside already came from re-rating M&S—from a struggling retailer to a leaner, more focused business. But just to jog your memory, the 'Reshape for Growth' consisted of the following:

  1. Review of the store portfolio, focusing on its core operations in the UK. This resulted in the closure of underperforming stores (mainly internationally) or renovation of old ones. M&S is also opening new, leaner stores and food halls.
  2. Supply chain optimization. This includes a £340 million investment in the construction of a new National Distribution Center (Northamptonshire) to maximize economies of scale, particularly in food (volumes are up more than 11% over the last three years).
  3. Increased private label initiatives for food, focusing on delivering great value-per-pound (over 500 product improvements and ~£30 million invested to lower price).
  4. Temporarily reduce shareholder returns by protecting cash flow and increasing Capex. Before the pandemic, they distributed ~£300 million in dividends, cutting them to zero from FY 2021 to FY 2023, and then returning to a much reduced dividend payment starting in FY 2025. They are currently distributing £60.5 million annually (almost 5x less than they paid before).
  5. M&S is deleveraging its balance sheet. When the 'Reshape for Growth' program began, the company had £1.48 billion in long-term debt, and they've managed to reduce it to £589.3 million (-60% so far). To give you an idea, debt/EBITDA fell from ~1.5x (which isn't high at all) to ~0.58x in FY 2025.

M&S says all these moves should add up to around £300 million in savings over three years. So far, they've already banked £120 million, which leaves roughly £180 million still on the table for the years ahead.

But let’s not forget, this isn’t M&S’s first rodeo when it comes to turnarounds. It's actually the second one this century.

And while their heavier push into Food puts them closer to players like Greggs (OTCPK:GGGSY) and Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDY), at the end of the day, they're still running a business that's exposed to the ups and downs of the macro environment.

In the late 1990s, M&S was the first British retailer to achieve £1 billion in pre-tax profit, even expanding into Asia

But then came the competition—mainly on price—and it hit them hard. What followed was a big restructuring: selling off Brooks Brothers (the oldest menswear brand in the U.S.) back in 2001, shutting down most stores across Continental Europe, Hong Kong, and Macau, and even fighting off a takeover attempt.

So, I recommend keeping your eyes open. Cyclicity is everything here.

RFG Year 3: What to Expect from Cash Flow and M&A?

The latest Annual Report made it quite clear: FY 2026 will have the highest Capex in the last decade (£600–650 million), by far the largest of the entire RFG. In addition to the new National Distribution Center in Northamptonshire (£340 million will be spent here alone), investments in technology infrastructure (to try to prevent cyberattacks like the ones in May, which are expected to eat into ~£295 million of profits), store maintenance/openings, and cost-cutting initiatives will also be made.

So, what can we expect from cash flow?

Wall St. estimates they should report revenue of ~£14.3 billion. If we use an EBITDA margin of ~7.5% (in line with the 5-year average), we're targeting an EBITDA of £1.07 billion.

Typically, the EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate is ~113% (average over the last 3 years), so I'm expecting an OCF of £1.2 billion—a reduction of at least £100 million year-over-year.

Basically, we should expect FCF between £420–470 million for FY 2026. Looking back, this is in line with the last two years, but it's a huge difference from FY 2022, when they reported almost £750 million in FCF.

Debt really isn't a concern anymore. It's down 60% since FY 2022, and both S&P and Moody's even bumped up their ratings. That means if M&S decides to pursue growth again (RFG is all about getting lean now so they can expand in FY 2027 and beyond), tapping debt markets will come at a much friendlier price.

Let's assume they keep it flat in FY 2026.

M&S is sitting on more than £1.14 billion in cash and short-term investments, plus another ~£590 million tucked away in long-term investments.

That's a serious cushion, basically the cherry on top.

As things stand, it feels like M&S could easily deliver solid returns to shareholders (perhaps even higher than pre-pandemic) while still having room to grow via M&A.

Do you doubt it? Let's dig a little deeper here.

We know that M&S ​​has a reasonably robust acquisition history, particularly of apparel brands: Brooks Brothers (1988), Per Una (2001), Jaeger (2021). Not to mention the joint venture with Ocado (OTCPK:OCDDY).

That said, we can say with some degree of conviction that M&S ​​will likely return to M&A once RFG's objectives are met.

And I see them targeting two types of companies:

  1. Apparel/Home Retail (mainly targets that have a significant overlap with Generation Z and a consolidated online presence); and
  2. Food Retail (consolidating its transition to a hybrid model).

When I think about the first category, boohoo (OTCPK:BHOOY), which owns brands like Nasty Gal, immediately comes to mind.

The rationale for this potential acquisition would be to boost M&A's online channels (currently ~30% of revenue) and integrate the two companies' marketplace model.

Boohoo has been experiencing serious problems recently (revenue of £790.3 million in FY 2025 is 62% lower than the top line in FY 2022, with EBITDA of -£65.7 million in the last report), failing to adapt to the post-pandemic consumer environment. Long-term debt isn't much of an issue either—just £122.9 million—so M&S could easily use it if they wanted to.

Assuming they pay 0.6x (slightly below what boohoo usually trades on a 5-year average), we'd be talking about an acquisition price of ~£400 million. M&S could afford this with its FCF alone (remember, I projected it to be £420–470 million in FY 2026, but it should grow in FY 2027).

Overall, this acquisition would be modestly accretive to revenue, but slightly dilutive to margins.

Now, if we shift over to Food Retail, the most obvious move would be for M&S to pursue the remaining 50% of Ocado Retail—their own joint venture with Ocado.

The rationale here is also simple: take full control of the operation and capitalize on the strong growth of the Food Retail segment alone (in FY 2025, the joint venture generated £3.09 billion, a 28% year-over-year growth).

Aiming for an EV/S of 2x (based on fast-growing online food businesses like Ocado itself), we would be targeting an acquisition price of ~£3 billion (since that's the remaining half). They would be able to finance this through an increase in debt, bringing the Debt/EBITDA ratio to something close to 3.3x even without any EBITDA growth (which I consider a very conservative scenario).

A final target that could expand M&S's private label portfolio (primarily with high-quality brands like Mr. Kipling and Ambrosia) is Premier Foods (OTCPK:PRRFY).

Unlike other targets, Premier would be accretive both in terms of revenue (£1.15 billion, +1% year-over-year) and EBITDA (£219.3 million, +0.7% year-over-year). This would lead to a premium of ~30% compared to the 8x EBITDA multiple the company is trading at.

I envision a deal of £2.16 billion. This would also be supported by increasing Debt/EBITDA to just over 2x given the high EBITDA accretion.

In summary:

  1. M&S is expected to generate FCF between £420–470 million for FY 2026, piling up even more cash if it continues to opt for organic growth and keeps debt flat year-over-year;
  2. If they decide to acquire companies, I see more sense in targeting Food Retail given the attractive multiples and accretive bottom line. This would also align with their ambition to expand their food offering, and doing so through private labels would be the smartest strategy;
  3. Even in the most extreme case (acquiring the other half of the joint venture at a high premium given the business's growth), M&S wouldn't go into debt that much. If they wanted, they could also rely on their existing cash or even ST Investments.

In my opinion, we should stick with the organic growth thesis for FY 2026, thinking about M&A only in FY 2027 when RFG has delivered a leaner M&S model and plenty of room to grow.

Returns: What to Expect?

Like I mentioned earlier, dividends aren’t really M&S’s top priority right now.

That said, they could technically go back to their FY 2019 payout (£303.5 million). The problem is that would eat up most of their expected FY 2025 FCF (£420–470 million), leaving them exposed if OCF takes a dip or if Capex ends up running higher than planned.

Right now, M&S is handing out about £60.5 million in dividends for FY 2025, and the plan is to bump that up by 20% in FY 2026. That works out to roughly 3.6p per share. With around 2 billion shares in circulation, you're looking at something close to £72 million in total dividends paid.

Looking at the ADR, we would be talking about ~$0.10 per share (considering that each ADR is equivalent to 2 shares).

I imagine they would continue pushing this into double digits in the short/medium term. If this continues over the next few years, we would see a distribution of £86.4 million for FY 2027 (3.6p per share), £103.6 million for FY 2028 (5.18p per share), and so on until normalization.

So, we're currently looking at a dividend yield of ~1% with the stock trading at $9.70.

It's also interesting to consider potential buybacks.

If M&S allocates somewhere between ~£80 and £160 million in buybacks for FY 2026 at an average price of £3.60, we would see a reduction of 22.2 to 44.4 million shares—delivering a return of ~1.1 to 2.2%.

Essentially, we're targeting a low-single-digit shareholder yield in the short/medium term. And very well covered by FCF.

Is It Time to Buy This Stock?

M&S is one of those interesting cases where the Street just can't agree. Analysts are pretty divided, and there's no real consensus on what the rating should be or what investors should do.

Quant range from 'Hold' to 'Sell,' with the latter standing out lately with downward EPS revisions.

My SA colleagues disagree, with ratings this year ranging from 'Buy' to 'Sell.'

Wall Street, on the other hand, seems to have a consensus on 'Strong Buy' and an upside of ~18% from now.

So, it's normal for investors to feel lost. I'll try to give my take on this.

In recent years, Marks and Spencer has been trading alongside other major retailers like Tesco and Sainsbury's (OTCQX:JSAIY). You can see this clearly in this chart:

In fact, they are currently trading above the 5-year average on metrics like EV/EBITDA, indicating that the market is paying a small premium to historical valuation due to RFG.

If we target ~7x EBITDA for FY 2026 (maintaining a premium to the historical average but still considering a discount relative to larger peers like Tesco), an EBITDA of £1.07 billion, 1.97 billion shares outstanding (applying a ~2% reduction from buybacks), and flat net debt year-over-year, we would have a price target of 390p–400p (upside of ~8–11% and a total return of less than 15% when considering dividends and buybacks).

This is my base case, with M&S continuing to perform well and preparing to pour money into buybacks/dividends and M&A for FY 2027 and beyond.

For a bear case, I estimate EBITDA at £950 million and the stock trading at an EV/EBITDA in line with the 5-year average of 6x (with no premium due to improvements in the RFG and weak macro outlook). I will also not consider buybacks. In this bear case, we would have a price target of ~300p (16.6% downside).

For a possible bull case, I would consider everything: £1.15 billion of EBITDA and buybacks. In this case, we would target 480p (~33% upside).

Looking at the ADR, we're talking about a price target of ~$10.40—essentially a ~6% upside by converting the base-case projected EBITDA to $1.44 million and using ~1 billion shares (remember, half of the shares outstanding). Again assuming low-digit dividends and buybacks, we'd be talking about a total return of less than 10%.

If we want to look further, we can take the consensus revenue for FY 2027 ($20.43 billion) and using an EBITDA margin of ~8%, we could expect an EBITDA of $1.63 billion.

Basically, we have two paths to follow here:

  1. Without M&A: Price target of $10, already bringing to present value at a discount rate of 8%.
  2. With M&A (simulation with Premier Foods): EBITDA of ~$1.9 billion, net debt of ~$1.8 billion. This would give a price target very close to $10.70, implying a mid-single-digit increase in the multiple, with the market pricing them more like a full-service retailer.

That said, M&S seems more like a 'Hold' to me at this point. The re-rating has already occurred (~300% over the last five years) and no longer presents us with an asymmetric opportunity—especially when you realize that it trades above its 5-year average for EV/EBITDA.

Basically, the market is already considering an attractive premium. The question here is whether it's enough.

In fact, in the last decade, M&S has only once had a higher multiple than Tesco. This was quickly corrected, and M&S has been trading below it ever since.

Right now, they're basically at that point already. Which makes me think the upside left is pretty limited—maybe mid-to-high single digits. In other words, you're probably looking at a return of less than 10% when adding dividends and buybacks.

It turns out they're particularly cash-rich, and to be honest, I understand why Wall St. is bullish: RFG is paying off, credit ratings are improving, M&A capacity is increasing, dividends are increasing, and other interesting things.

But could this be the much-talked-about momentum bias? After all, Wall St. tends to extrapolate past valuation trends quite frequently.

Remember that turnarounds have diminishing returns. And, as much as I believe they're outperforming traditional retail, I don't see much room for asymmetric returns.

So, hold on to M&S and enjoy the dividends, which should continue to grow by double digits from now on. I'll be keeping an eye on this retail giant's performance and its M&A options.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Blue Chip Of Real Estate, 5.4% Paid Monthly: Realty Income
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

You'd have to be hiding under a rock if you weren't aware of the large push within America to see the Federal Reserve cut interest rates.

Many are cheering on the idea that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates as a way to stimulate or boost the economy. However, what they seem to overlook or potentially forget is that the only reason the Federal Reserve is inclined to cut interest rates is that the U.S. economy is weakening. You don't cut interest rates on a strong economy because you may put it into overdrive and overheat the economy, which would worsen inflation.

In its simplest definition, inflation is more dollars chasing fewer goods. If the government were to print $5 trillion and hand it out to everyday Americans, there would be an economy awash with extra cash but not extra production. Once people go out and start spending that money, they would be driving up prices because the availability of goods would be the same and then become scarcer as more money was chasing it. We saw this with the COVID-era stimulus payments. There would be no inflation if the government printed $5 trillion and then held it in its bank account and did nothing with it, because inflation only worsens when money circulates in the economy, chasing goods and services.

This is why higher interest rates slow inflation down. When it becomes more expensive to borrow money and more rewarding to save it, then money stops moving as quickly. The velocity of money in the market slows down because investors, retirees, and everyday Americans are then incentivized to hold on to their money and save it and not spend it or borrow it.

Conversely, once interest rates are cut, money starts flowing more rapidly again, and this can help stimulate the economy by increasing demand for goods. Thus, you only stimulate an economy when it is weakening.

Unfortunately, we do see a lot of doublespeak here, where some are saying the economy is strong and robust, yet also want interest rates to be cut to stimulate it further. Inflation and jobs data from the government show that the economy is indeed slowing down and weakening. The long-called-for recession may finally be arriving.

What this means is that an income investor like myself and potentially like you, if you're reading this today, needs to have investments in our portfolios that will benefit when interest rates are cut but also have investments in your portfolio that work as an anchor in rough seas, providing strong and steady income and returns regardless of what's going on in the economy and the market around them.

Today, I want to look at one of those anchor investments in my portfolio.

Let's dive in!

An Anchor in Rough Seas

Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O), yielding 5.4%, is a triple-net lease REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) that focuses on buying properties and leasing them out to well-known and established companies, who take care of all maintenance, insurance, and taxes for that specific property.

Note: We've covered O previously, and it has beaten the market (SPX) ever since. This article should be viewed as an update on that article.

O recently announced its 132nd dividend increase since becoming a public company. On its own, the dividend increase isn't very large. It is an increase of approximately 0.18% higher than the dividend paid the prior month. However, the sheer number of these small increases and the occasional large increase add up. O's October dividend in 2025 will be 2.27% higher than the dividend it paid in October 2024.

O is a prime example of the power of compounding income. Investors have seen their incomes grow slowly and steadily, with O frequently hiking its dividend more than four times a year.

O is able to produce steady income growth thanks to a business model that is highly reliable and has gone through stock market bubbles, recessions, COVID, and various rate environments. Through it all, O has seen its AFFO per share decline only once, in 2009. Source

Consistency in operations supports consistency in dividends. O is able to drive this consistency by having long-term leases with stable tenants. O's current weighted-average lease term is approximately 9.0 years, with less than 10% of its portfolio expiring in any given year:

O has become one of the largest publicly traded REITs, acquiring several large peers in recent years, including VEREIT and Spirit Capital. This has made O the largest REIT in the triple-net sector, boasting a market cap of $55 billion, with W. P. Carey (WPC) a distant second at $15 billion.

With great scale comes new opportunities. O has a strong platform of real estate professionals who are constantly evaluating investment opportunities for O. A lot of those opportunities, O ultimately passes on.

A major reason that O chooses to pass on opportunities is that the Year 1 yield isn't high enough. Running a public company, O wants every acquisition to be accretive to the bottom line right off the bat. They want to grow earnings for common shareholders every year, and that means passing up on opportunities that might have great long-term returns but don't have the upfront revenue.

In the Q&A of the Q2 earnings call, CFO Pong explained that this is one of the reasons O is looking to branch into managing private capital:

"The type of return thresholds, we've talked about this before, what's right for private capital is something that has a great IRR profile over the long term, 10 years plus, but may not necessarily have that year 1 yield.

And of that $3-plus billion that we talked about, that was the primary reason why we had to pass on it. And so the expansion of the buy box by virtue of having tools like this that aren't thinking about this notion of year 1 investment spread, or they're thinking about true underwriting real estate over the long term and thinking about it from an IRR standpoint. We're not sacrificing IRR. There's just a different trajectory towards it. And that's really how we're thinking about what will go to private capital."

Private capital is a potential solution to this issue because O can provide much less of its own equity. Instead of owning 100% of the property, O will own 20% and will receive a management fee in addition to the rent its equity ownership is entitled to.

O receives the fee regardless of the current cash flow for the underlying property—solving the problem where Year 1 cash flow might be below O's target.

O is already looking at these deals anyway, sourcing $66 billion in the first half and closing on only $2.5 billion.

Management is suggesting there was approximately $3 billion in volume that wasn't appropriate for the REIT to invest in alone but would have an attractive investment profile for private capital, which is less concerned about immediate cash flow and more concerned with the long-term IRR over 10+ years.

By having both options available to them, O broadens its opportunities to generate cash and grow its dividend for us. As with anything new, there are always plenty of detractors and investors who get nervous about it. And in all fairness, there is always a risk that any new venture won't work out. However, O hasn't grown into O because it bought a Taco Bell in 1969 and never changed a thing. O has innovated, it has grown, and it has seized opportunities—frequently being a trailblazer in the industry.

As O sets out on this new chapter, we know things will change. One thing we are confident won't change is that O will pay a monthly dividend, and it will continue to provide us with numerous dividend increases every.

Conclusion

The Federal Reserve has resumed rate cuts, and O is well-positioned to benefit. Whenever the economy weakens, many investors will flock to what are considered "safe harbor" investments. O solidly falls into this category. Furthermore, REITs are viewed as bond alternatives and often will trade based on their yield more than on the performance of the company itself when compared to peers. What this means is that when interest rates are cut, we can expect that O's share price will climb accordingly, reducing its yield available on the market. This means that we have a prime window right now to buy into an anchor investment that will benefit our portfolio as the economy weakens and interest rates fall because of its position of strength and its REIT status.

The wonderful thing about the High Dividend Opportunities Model Portfolio is that it targets a 9% yield on average. We're able to hold investments like O in our model portfolio because we have other higher-yielding, higher-risk investments, creating a blended yield of that 9%. O provides a wonderful contribution to the total return of our model portfolio and a steady, no-nonsense income stream every single month.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.
